summary: Joe Reis shares his weekly roundup of tech news and commentary while managing a heavy workload of courses, articles, and his upcoming data modeling book. The post covers major developments including Databricks' new DBRX LLM, the Redis licensing controversy, AI hallucination risks in code copilots, and the DOJ's antitrust lawsuit against Apple.
key_points: Databricks' DBRX LLM positions them ahead in the AI race, validating their MosaicML acquisition
Redis licensing change exemplifies risks of corporate-backed open source projects
AI code copilots create security risks through hallucinated package names that attackers can exploit
The US v. Apple lawsuit is compared to US v. Microsoft and will have massive tech industry implications
Prediction that AI will crash the influencer ecosystem, leading to a resurgence of in-person 'meatspace' interactions
money_quotes: Yet another case of why corporate-backed open source is amazing until they decide to change the rules.

There should be some pretty interesting and potentially nasty feedback loops with AI outputs and re-training, especially as developers become more and more disconnected from the code they're responsible for.
If you're delivering value, you're probably busy delivering it instead of talking about it.
I predict that in-person 'meatspace' will become popular again, along with its version of influencers.
This is the US vs Microsoft for the 2020s, and the implications will be massive for the tech industry.
key_insight: The tech industry faces a tension between AI's impressive capabilities and its practical risks, from corporate open-source bait-and-switches to dangerous feedback loops in AI-assisted development.
